learning_outcomes.description: Understand core theories, principles, and practices of offender rehabilitation, re-entry and reintegration;
Demonstrate enhanced skills of critical thinking and reflection in diverse contexts;
Demonstrate enhanced communication employable in diverse settings; and
Demonstrate capacity to review, evaluate and apply evidence for achieving best practice outcomes.

assessments.due_date:
In class test 1a – RNR and Good Lives Models (15%) 18/08/2026 10:40 am

In class test 1b – Offender rehabilitation (15%) 15/09/2026 10:40 am
assessments.task_description:
Students are required to complete a series of in-class tests throughout the course. These tests will be conducted under closed-book conditions during scheduled class time.

  • Test Schedule: Weeks 4 and 8
  • Start Time: 9:40 am for each test. Students should be in class and ready to begin the test by 9.30. This will provide time for students to ask the course coordinator any questions.
  • Duration: 60 minutes per test. The test will take 45 minutes to complete but extra time has been factored into the completion time.

A regular class session will take place prior to each test on the same day.

On the day of each assessment, students will be provided with the test paper and an answer booklet. Each test will consist of 3 questions designed to assess the application of course content. The tests are structured to progressively build on key concepts and frameworks covered in the course. All students' bags should be placed to the side of the class. Phones should be visible and placed face down on the desk.

In class test 1a – RNR and Good Lives Models (15%) Week 4: 18-8-26

In class test 1b – Offender rehabilitation (15%) Week 8: 15-9-26


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.due_date:
16/10/2026 5:00 pm

Note: Attendance of a guest speaker presentation is a requirement for this assessment. Students can submit this assessment any time after their chosen guest speaker presentation, up to 5pm, Friday of Week 11.

Students who do not attend the guest speaker presentation will receive 0 marks for their submission (e.g. if you submit the summary and reflection but you did not attend that guest speaker’s presentation, this will result in a zero mark for this submission). It is the student's responsibility to ensure that their attendance is recorded.
assessments.task_description:
Throughout the semester, a series of guest speakers from organisations such as Queensland Corrective Services and Victoria Police will present in lectures. These presentations provide important insights into contemporary correctional practice and offender rehabilitation.

Students are expected to attend these sessions and select one (1) guest speaker presentation to analyse. You can choose any guest speaker. You are required to produce a concise, critical summary and reflection (maximum 3 pages) addressing the questions outlined below. 

Your response should be structured into the following sections:

Section 1: Understanding the Presentation

Provide a clear and accurate summary of the presentation by addressing the following:

·        Who was the guest speaker (e.g., role, professional background, organisation)?

·        What is the main focus of their work?

·        Identify and briefly explain 2–3 key points emphasised by the speaker.

·        What types of evidence, case studies, or professional experiences did the speaker draw upon?

Section 2: Critical Analysis

Demonstrate critical engagement with the presentation:

·        What do you interpret as the speaker’s overall message regarding corrections and offender rehabilitation?

·        What were the strongest aspects of the speaker’s arguments or insights? Explain why they were effective or persuasive.

Section 3: Reflection

Reflect on your learning and perspective:

·        What is one key insight you gained from this presentation?

·        Did the presentation challenge or reinforce your views on corrections or offender rehabilitation? Explain your response with reference to specific points from the presentation.

Assessment Requirements:

Length: Maximum of 3 pages.

Formatting: 1.5 spacing; 12 p font.

Structure: Use clear headings and subheadings corresponding to each section

Referencing: Not required, unless you draw on course materials (in which case, use appropriate academic referencing).

Due Date: Submit any time by Week 11

Attendance is a requirement for this assessment. Students who do not attend the guest speaker presentation will receive 0 marks for their submission (e.g. if you submit the summary and reflection but you did not attend that guest speaker’s presentation, this will result in a zero mark for this submission). It is the student's responsibility to ensure that their attendance is recorded.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students are required to complete a case and risk formulation based on an assigned offender profile, using the HCR-20 V3 risk assessment framework.

For this assessment, you will:

  • Apply the HCR-20 V3 framework to analyse the offender profile
  • Develop a structured case formulation integrating relevant risk factors
  • Arrive at a final risk judgement regarding the offender’s likelihood of future violent offending.
  • Propose scenario planning and risk management strategies to mitigate or reduce the risk of reoffending, particularly in the context of potential parole


Assessment Requirements:

  • Maximum length: 1,500 words (excluding references).
  • Format: 1.5 spaced, 12 point font.
  • Structure: Use clear headings and subheadings to organise key sections.
  • Referencing: Include a reference list at the end of the report.


Students must select ONE of two offender profiles, which will be made available on the course learning site.

This assessment requires you to apply a structured professional risk assessment tool to a hypothetical case. It is designed to reflect work-integrated learning, simulating real-world practice in offender risk assessment and management.

Relevant materials and guidance for completing this task are available on the course learning site. To assist students with this assessment, Week 12 will be dedicated to reviewing the assessment requirements, covering examples of case and risk formulations, and providing students with the opportunity to discuss their plans or drafts in consultation with the course coordinator.


AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.

1: Indigenous offenders; 2: Rehabilitation challenges

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04

The rehabilitation and release of terrorist offenders

This seminar is divided into two sections: 1: Covers rehabilitation and risk as it relates to Indigenous offenders; 2: Examines a range of rehabilitation challenges pertaining to engaging offenders and facilitating desistance.
